food_subgroup: Cookie and biscuit crumbs / dessert toppings

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Oreo crumbs or crushed sandwich cookies; closest parent 'cookie (food product)' used as ontology anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Biscuits and cookies) is the closest defensible parent category for Oreo crumbs; no specific code exists for crushed/crumbled sandwich cookies.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to Oreo crumbs or crushed sandwich cookie product; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.description: 1 tablespoon (approx. 10 g) as a dessert topping
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Oreo crumbs as a dessert topping is approximately 10 g (one tablespoon), used to garnish churros, ice cream, cakes, or beverages.

nutrient_source.title: Cookies, chocolate sandwich, with creme filling (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB 18158)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Oreo crumbs are mechanically crushed Oreo sandwich cookies. Nutrient values are taken from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for 'Cookies, chocolate sandwich, with creme filling' (NDB 18158 / FDC ID 174928), which is the standard reference for Oreo-type cookies. Crushing does not materially alter macronutrient composition per 100 g; values are therefore directly applicable.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal) / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Oreo crumbs specifically. GI of 71 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for chocolate sandwich cookies (Oreo-type), consistent with values reported in Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283 for plain sweet biscuits/cookies (GI range 55–77, reference glucose=100). A value of 71 is used as the best defensible analogous estimate. Glycemic load per 10 g serving = (71 × 7.13 g available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = 5.1.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (476 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should strictly limit Oreo crumbs as a topping, as even a small 10 g serving contributes approximately 48 mg sodium, and cumulative use across desserts can meaningfully elevate daily sodium intake toward the 2,000 mg recommended limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (71) and high sugar content (40.4 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Oreo crumbs carry a high GI of 71 and contain 40.4 g of sugar per 100 g, making them a high-glycemic topping that can cause rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly minimise use and account for carbohydrate load within their meal plan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (5.6 g per 100 g) and high sugar content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Oreo crumbs contain 5.6 g saturated fat and 40.4 g sugar per 100 g; regular use as a dessert topping may contribute to dyslipidaemia and elevated cardiovascular risk, and should be limited in individuals managing cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Oreo crumbs are finely crushed Oreo chocolate sandwich cookies, produced by mechanically grinding the iconic black-and-white cream-filled biscuit manufactured by Nabisco (Mondelez International). The crumbs consist of dark cocoa-flavoured wafer fragments and white creme filling pieces, yielding a coarse, slightly powdery texture with a sweet, chocolatey flavour and a faint vanilla note from the creme. In Singapore's dessert scene, Oreo crumbs are widely used as a topping or garnish on churros, soft-serve ice cream, bubble tea, cakes, and other desserts, including at Spanish-themed dessert outlets such as Churros Republic. Nutritionally, Oreo crumbs are an ultra-processed, energy-dense ingredient: per 100 g they provide approximately 480 kcal, 71.3 g carbohydrate (of which 40.4 g is sugar), 20.1 g fat (5.6 g saturated), 4.7 g protein, and 476 mg sodium. Their glycemic index is high (GI ≈ 71), making them a concern for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or insulin resistance. The high sodium content is relevant for hypertension management. They contain wheat and soy allergens and are not gluten-free. A typical serving as a dessert topping is approximately 10 g (one tablespoon), contributing around 48 kcal and 7.1 g carbohydrate per use.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Oreo crumbs?
Are Oreo crumbs high in sugar?
What is the GI of Oreo cookies?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for chocolate sandwich cookies with creme filling (NDB 18158), which is the standard reference analogue for Oreo cookies. Crushing does not alter per-100 g macronutrient composition. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) for Oreo-type cookies. No Singapore-specific HPB or ICMR-NIN entry exists for this product. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 18158 (Cookies, chocolate sandwich, with creme filling), the standard reference for Oreo-type cookies. Crushing does not materially alter per-100 g composition. 2. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0; this is confirmed zero per USDA source (reformulated product). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 confirmed zero per USDA source (no animal-derived fat in wafer). vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 confirmed zero per USDA source. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0 confirmed zero per USDA source. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 confirmed zero per USDA source (no animal products). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 confirmed zero per USDA source. 3. GI of 71 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care for chocolate sandwich cookies; no direct GI measurement for Oreo crumbs exists in the literature. Glycemic load per 10 g serving = 5.1. 4. The cuisine tag 'Spanish' in the input reflects the restaurant context (Churros Republic) rather than the food's origin; Oreo crumbs are a US-origin commercial product and have been reclassified accordingly. 5. Halal status is not confirmed for Oreo products sold in Singapore without explicit MUIS certification; is_halal is set to false pending verification. 6. FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03301710 (cookie food product) is used as closest parent; no exact FoodOn term exists for Oreo crumbs. FoodEx2 code A00LC (Biscuits and cookies) is closest parent. 7. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean context (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as the food is served in Singapore. 8. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, water_g are all reported from USDA FoodData Central NDB 18158.

improvement_actions_taken: Reclassified cuisine from 'Spanish' (restaurant context) to 'Western / International' (product origin) with explanation in notes.
Added multilingual local names and aliases for Singapore context (Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi).
Sourced nutrient values from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 18158 as approved secondary source.
Documented GI 71 as analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. (2008) with full estimation basis.
Confirmed zero values (trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b12_mcg, vitamin_d_mcg) documented in notes as confirmed zero per USDA source.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases list are perfectly consistent — all language-specific strings appear verbatim in flat aliases.
Applied correct ontology match_type values (closest_parent, none) per allowed vocabulary.
Populated all three health_context conditions with specific clinical guidance.
